reasoning: The taint path is fully confirmed end-to-end. (1) WRITE side: Metabox_Taxonomy::save() at line 124 gates on current_user_can($tax_obj->cap->edit_terms, $term_id). For built-in taxonomies (category, post_tag), edit_terms resolves to manage_categories, which is held by the Editor role — not Administrator — on standard WordPress single-site. No unfiltered_html check is present anywhere in the save path. (2) SANITIZATION: Common::sanitize_hfc_data() passes head/body/footer fields to sanitize_html_with_scripts(). That function uses a preg_replace_callback to extract full <script>...</script> blocks into placeholders before wp_kses() runs. Only the opening-tag attributes of each script block are passed through wp_kses() with the allowed attribute list (src, async, defer, type, nonce, etc. — no event handlers). The inner JavaScript body is never touched and is stored verbatim via update_term_meta(). (3) OUTPUT sink: class-front.php inject_code() retrieves the stored code via Common::get_meta_auto(), builds the output string in build_output(), then executes: echo $output (line 152-154). An explicit comment at line 155-156 reads 'We do not use wp_kses($output, $this->allowed_html); because that would escape < > and & which is already sanitized on entry.' The raw stored JavaScript is echoed directly into every taxonomy archive page that matches the term. (4) FULL TAINT PATH: $_POST['auhfc']['head'/'body'/'footer'] (Editor submits taxonomy edit form) -> sanitize_hfc_data() -> sanitize_html_with_scripts() (script body preserved verbatim) -> update_term_meta() -> get_meta_auto() on taxonomy archive request -> build_output() -> echo $output (unescaped). The path is unbroken. (5) ADVERSARIAL CHECK: The opening-tag attribute sanitization via wp_kses() does remove disallowed attributes like onload/onerror from the <script> tag itself, so inline event-handler bypass on the tag is blocked. However, this is irrelevant because the script body is fully preserved — an attacker simply places alert(document.cookie) inside <script>...</script> and it executes. The nonce check and CSRF protection are valid but only prevent cross-site request forgery; they do not prevent a logged-in Editor from intentionally storing malicious JS. (6) CAPABILITY BOUNDARY: WordPress core requires unfiltered_html (administrator-only on single-site) before wp_kses passes raw script content in post meta or content. This plugin uses a lower capability (edit_terms) for taxonomy term meta containing raw JS, creating a privilege boundary below the WordPress-standard gate. This is the core issue: it is by design for the plugin's feature, but the design choice lowers the minimum trust level required to store executable JS that runs for all visitors on taxonomy archive pages.
